1: When the Lamb opens the seventh seal, there is silence in heaven for about half an hour — the reverential hush before the climactic judicial act. The Lamb's authority over the seventh seal is total: what he opens, none can close (Revelation 3:7). The silence is the creation's response to the presence of the one who bears both the marks of the cross and the authority of the cosmos — the pregnant pause before the word of Christ's judgment is spoken.
2: Seven angels are given seven trumpets — the instruments of divine proclamation and judgment (Joshua 6:1-21: the trumpets at Jericho; 1 Thessalonians 4:16: 'the trumpet call of God' accompanies Christ's return). The seven trumpets proceed from the Lamb's opening of the seventh seal — the entire trumpet sequence is Christ's executive action in history. The angels are instruments; Christ is the executive authority behind each blast.
3: The angel at the golden altar with incense — Hebrews 7:25 (Christ 'always lives to make intercession' for those who come to God through him) and Hebrews 9:24 (he 'entered heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf'). The incense offering before God's throne embodies the priestly intercession that the NT identifies with Christ's ongoing high-priestly ministry. The prayers of the saints are received through the mediating work of the one who offered himself as the ultimate sacrifice.
4: The smoke of incense with the prayers of the saints rising before God — Christ's intercession carries the prayers of his people to the Father. Romans 8:34 ('Christ Jesus... is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us') and John 17 (the high-priestly prayer) are the Gospel and epistolary background. The martyrs' cry of 'how long?' (Revelation 6:10) is among the prayers rising through this incense — Christ carries their cry to the Father's throne.
5: Fire from the altar hurled to the earth — the altar that received the saints' prayers and Christ's intercession now becomes the source of divine judgment. The intercessory altar and the judicial altar are the same: Christ's priestly intercession and his judicial authority are unified in the one mediator (1 Timothy 2:5). The prayers for justice (Revelation 6:10) answered in judgment (fire on the earth) show that intercession and judgment are both Christ's to exercise.
6: The seven angels prepare to sound their trumpets — the entire trumpet sequence proceeds from the Lamb's seal-opening (Revelation 8:1) and the altar-prayer dynamic (Revelation 8:3-5). The full sequence of historical judgments is under Christ's executive authority. No trumpet sounds without his governance; no judgment falls outside the scope of his opened scroll. The preparatory pause emphasizes the deliberateness and authority of what follows.
7: The first trumpet — hail and fire mixed with blood hurled onto the earth. This is the seventh plague of Egypt (Exodus 9:13-35) redeployed as an eschatological Exodus judgment. Christ executes the new-Exodus judgment on the anti-Exodus empire (Rome = the new Egypt in Revelation 11:8). As Moses mediated the Exodus plagues against Egypt that refused to release God's people, so Christ mediates the eschatological plagues against Rome that refuses to release the church.
8: The second trumpet — a blazing mountain thrown into the sea. Jeremiah 51:25 pronounces doom on Babylon as 'a destroying mountain' that God will make 'a burnt-out mountain' — applied now to Rome as the new Babylon. 'A third of the sea turned to blood' echoes the first Exodus plague (Exodus 7:20-21). The fractional 'third' measure throughout the trumpet sequence demonstrates that Christ's judgment has proportionality: severe but measured, not indiscriminate destruction.
9: A third of the sea creatures died and a third of the ships destroyed. The judgment falls on Rome's commercial empire — its seaborne trade network (the merchants, sea captains, and sailors who mourned Rome's fall in Revelation 18:17-19). Christ's judgment is targeted: the commercial system that exploited the nations and fed on their labor is the specific object of the sea-judgment.
10: The third trumpet — a great star blazing like a torch falls on the rivers and springs, poisoning them. Jeremiah 9:15 (God will make the people 'eat bitter food and drink poisoned water' as judgment for apostasy) is the background. Christ promised living water to all who come to him (John 4:10-14; Revelation 7:37-38); the poisoning of earthly waters in judgment contrasts with the unpoisoned living water he provides. Those who rejected the water of life now drink from waters made bitter by judgment.
11: The star named Wormwood causes many to die from the bitter waters. John 6:53-58: Jesus declared that those who do not eat his flesh and drink his blood have no life in them; the bitter waters of judgment are the reversal of the cup of salvation (Psalms 116:13) that Christ offers. The bitter cup that he drank on the cross (Mark 14:36: 'take this cup from me') redeems those who would otherwise drink only from bitter waters.
12: The fourth trumpet — a third of the sun, moon, and stars darkened. Amos 8:9; Joel 2:31; and Matthew 24:29 (Jesus quoting these cosmic signs in connection with the coming of the Son of Man). The darkening of the lights echoes John 1:5 (the light shines in the darkness) — Christ is the light that cannot ultimately be extinguished; these are penultimate darkenings, not the final light-out. 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12) remains true even when the created lights are darkened.
13: The eagle flying in midheaven crying 'Woe, woe, woe' — the three woes are the anti-beatitudes: instead of 'blessed' there is 'woe.' Christ pronounced beatitudes on the poor in spirit, the mourning, the meek (Matthew 5:3-12); he also pronounced woes on those who rejected his kingdom (Matthew 23:13-36). The eagle's cry signals that what follows surpasses what came before — the remaining trumpets are directed specifically at those who refuse repentance.
